display_name: STP port role (root/designated/alternate) and port state (forwarding/blocking) are different things
context: Reading 'show spanning-tree' output and seeing a port listed as Alternate/Blocking or Root/Forwarding
observation: A port shows role=Alternate with state=Blocking, or role=Designated with state=Forwarding
naive_interpretation: A port in Blocking state is broken or misconfigured and should be fixed to reach Forwarding; any non-forwarding port is a problem
correct_interpretation: Role describes WHY the port is in a given state; state describes WHAT the port is doing with frames. An Alternate port in Blocking state is STP working correctly — it is a redundant path being suppressed to prevent loops. A Designated port in Forwarding state is the elected forwarder for that segment. Blocking is not a fault; it is the mechanism that prevents L2 loops. The role determines whether blocking is intentional (Alternate/Backup = expected) or indicates a problem (Designated port stuck in Blocking/Learning = convergence issue). Fix convergence problems, not intentional blocking.
why: RSTP (802.1w) separated the concept of role (root, designated, alternate, backup) from state (discarding, learning, forwarding) to clarify STP behavior
An Alternate port is a backup path to the root bridge — it blocks by design and takes over if the Root port fails, providing sub-second failover
A Backup port is a redundant path on the same segment (same switch, two ports to same hub/shared segment) — extremely rare in modern networks
Only when a Designated or Root port is NOT in Forwarding state is there a potential convergence problem worth investigating

confirmation_commands.purpose: See role and state side by side for all ports to identify which blocking ports are Alternate (expected) vs problematic
Get a count of forwarding/blocking/listening/learning ports across all VLANs for a quick health check
See topology change counts and last change time to identify convergence issues vs stable blocking
Check a specific port's role, state, cost, and designated bridge to understand why it is blocking

traps: Trying to 'fix' an Alternate/Blocking port by lowering cost or changing priority when it is correctly preventing a loop
Counting total blocking ports as a problem metric — a well-designed redundant L2 network SHOULD have Alternate ports in Blocking
Confusing legacy STP states (Blocking/Listening/Learning/Forwarding) with RSTP roles — legacy STP merged role and state into one concept
Not investigating when a Designated or Root port is stuck in Learning or Discarding — this IS a convergence problem, unlike Alternate/Blocking which is healthy
